primary_diagnosis.name: Peripheral enteropathic arthritis with concurrent acute Crohn's disease flare

differential.diagnosis: enteropathic (IBD-associated) peripheral arthritis with Crohn's flare
differential.evidence_basis: Concurrent gut-and-joint flare in an established Crohn's patient who has missed maintenance biologic — the classic type-1 peripheral enteropathic arthropathy pattern, which parallels bowel activity.
Warm fluctuant joint effusions demand exclusion; but septic arthritis is typically monoarticular with a high spiking fever, not symmetrical polyarticular temporally locked to a bowel flare.
Crohn's raises stricture/obstruction risk, so it must be screened; but active diarrhoea and flatus with a soft, non-rigid abdomen argue strongly against it.
Acute hot joints raise crystal disease; but the polyarticular large-joint distribution locked to an active IBD flare is atypical for gout.

differential.diagnosis: enteropathic peripheral arthritis with Crohn's flare
differential.evidence_basis: Red-flag screen negative: no single fiery hot joint, no spiking fever/rigors (against sepsis); still passing diarrhoea and flatus with a soft non-rigid abdomen (against obstruction). The gut-joint concurrence in known Crohn's confirms the enteropathic pattern.

Symmetrical polyarthritis without a dominant hot joint or high fever is not the septic pattern; empirical over-treatment as sepsis would be an error, though the warm effusions still warrant in-person review and, if any doubt, aspiration.
Ongoing diarrhoea and flatus with a soft abdomen and no vomiting exclude obstruction clinically.

differential.diagnosis: peripheral enteropathic arthritis with concurrent Crohn's flare, complicated by mild dehydration and functional immobility
differential.evidence_basis: Known Crohn's + missed biologic + concurrent bowel-and-large-joint flare with a negative acute-red-flag screen. The management task is flare control (systemic corticosteroid, restart/adjust biologic), rehydration and mobility/social support — an urgent outpatient/same-day pathway, not an emergency.

red_flags.description: Warm fluctuant joint effusions — must exclude septic arthritis
red_flags.clinical_significance: A genuinely septic joint is a surgical emergency; here the polyarticular, afebrile, IBD-linked pattern argues against it, but the possibility mandates in-person assessment and a low threshold for joint aspiration if any single joint declares itself.
Absence of high spiking fever and rigors lowers the probability of joint sepsis and systemic bacterial infection.

red_flags.description: Rigid/board-like abdomen, absent flatus, or faeculent vomiting (surgical abdomen / obstruction)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A soft abdomen still passing diarrhoea and flatus without vomiting excludes obstruction and peritonitis — the alarming 'surgical abdomen' framing is not borne out.

red_flags.description: Mild dehydration with inability to maintain safe oral hydration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing volume loss from diarrhoea plus poor intake and immobility can progress; it is currently mild (normal BP, only mild tachycardia) but drives the need for prompt same-day review and rehydration, and mandatory safety-netting for worsening dehydration.

red_flags.description: Functional collapse — bedbound, living alone, cannot reach the toilet, high fall risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Social/functional acuity: an unsupported patient who cannot hydrate, mobilise or toilet safely alone is not safely managed by a remote-only plan and needs urgent face-to-face support even though the underlying diagnosis is outpatient-managed.

pathophysiology_summary: In inflammatory bowel disease, gut mucosal inflammation drives a systemic immune-mediated (spondyloarthritis-spectrum) process. Type-1 peripheral enteropathic arthropathy is pauci/oligoarticular, asymmetric-to-symmetric, affects large lower-limb joints (knees, ankles), is self-limiting and PARALLELS bowel disease activity — so a bowel flare and a joint flare occur together, as here. Loss of maintenance biologic control (missed dose) permits both compartments to flare. The warm effusions are sterile inflammatory synovitis, not infection. Volume loss from diarrhoea plus reduced intake produces mild dehydration. The definitive management is control of the underlying IBD flare (systemic corticosteroid, reinstatement/adjustment of biologic under gastroenterology), rehydration and supportive mobility/social care — NOT antibiotics or emergency surgery. NSAIDs, a reflexive analgesic choice for hot joints, are relatively contraindicated because they can worsen the IBD flare.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the 'rule out bowel obstruction / rigid abdomen' framing into an ED/000 escalation when the abdomen is soft and the patient is still passing diarrhoea and flatus — over-triage of a bowel flare.

Treating warm effusions as sepsis and escalating to emergency IV antibiotics/surgery, ignoring the symmetrical polyarticular, afebrile, IBD-locked pattern that points to sterile enteropathic synovitis.
Reaching for an NSAID to settle the hot joints — relatively contraindicated in an active IBD flare because it can worsen bowel inflammation and bleeding.

Recognising the flare but under-supporting the patient: leaving a dehydrated, bedbound, socially-isolated person on a routine 48-hour outpatient plan without arranging urgent same-day review, hydration and fall/social risk mitigation.
Attempting to 'manage the flare at home' with advice alone when the correct action requires parenteral therapy, rehydration and biologic reinstatement that telehealth cannot deliver.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the abdomen (softness, guarding, rigidity, tenderness distribution)
Direct examination/palpation of the joints for warmth, effusion and fluctuance
Blood pressure, respiratory rate and true oxygen saturation without a device
Joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis to definitively exclude septic arthritis
Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR/faecal calprotectin), electrolytes and renal function for dehydration
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a known-Crohn's flare with enteropathic arthritis; arrange URGENT SAME-DAY face-to-face medical review (GP, urgent care or home/community rapid-response) for hydration assessment, parenteral therapy and hands-on exclusion of a septic joint, PLUS urgent gastroenterology referral within 48 hours to restart/adjust the biologic; give strong deterioration safety-netting. Do NOT escalate to ED/000 as a surgical abdomen or joint sepsis, and do NOT recommend an NSAID.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (concurrent gut-and-joint flare in known Crohn's is a recognisable pattern). The dominant scored risk here is OVER-action — escalating an outpatient flare to ED/000, or treating sterile synovitis as sepsis — with a secondary risk of under-supporting the dehydration and social/functional collapse.
